title: Quiz: Multi-Channel Synchronization and Timing
description: Test your understanding of clock distribution, timing jitter, phase synchronization, and scalable synchronization architectures for quantum control systems

questions.question: For a 5 GHz qubit, what is the timing precision required to achieve 1 degree of phase accuracy?

options.text: About 56 picoseconds
About 5.6 picoseconds
About 0.56 picoseconds
About 0.056 picoseconds

questions.explanation: At 5 GHz, one full period is 200 ps. One degree of phase is 1/360 of a period: 200 ps / 360 = 0.56 ps. This illustrates why sub-picosecond timing stability is required for high-fidelity quantum gates.

questions.question: In a hierarchical clock distribution system, why are equal cable lengths important?

options.text: To reduce cost by using the same cable part number
To minimize timing skew between channels by equalizing propagation delays
To simplify cable management
To improve signal amplitude uniformity

questions.explanation: Equal cable lengths ensure equal propagation delays to all channels, minimizing timing skew. Typical coaxial cables have propagation delay of about 5 ns/m, so even 1 cm difference creates 50 ps of skew, which can be significant for quantum operations.

questions.question: When multiple independent jitter sources contribute to timing uncertainty, how do they combine?

options.text: They add linearly: total = source1 + source2 + ...
They add in quadrature (RSS): total = sqrt(source1^2 + source2^2 + ...)
Only the largest source matters; others are negligible
They multiply: total = source1 * source2 * ...

questions.explanation: Independent (uncorrelated) jitter sources add in quadrature (root-sum-square). This is a statistical property of uncorrelated random variables. Total_rms = sqrt(jitter1^2 + jitter2^2 + ...). This means reducing the largest source has the most impact on total jitter.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) in a quantum control system?

options.text: To amplify weak signals
To convert analog signals to digital
To maintain a fixed phase relationship between local oscillators and the master clock
To measure qubit state populations

questions.explanation: PLLs synchronize the phase of output signals (local oscillators) to a reference signal (master clock). This ensures all control channels maintain coherent phase relationships, which is essential for multi-qubit gates where relative phases between channels determine gate fidelity.

questions.question: Digital delay correction in an FPGA can completely eliminate timing skew between channels regardless of the magnitude of the skew.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Digital delay correction can only adjust in increments of the sample period (e.g., 1 ns for a 1 GSPS DAC). Any skew that isn't an integer multiple of the sample period leaves a residual timing error. Additionally, very large skews may exceed the available delay buffer depth.

questions.question: Why is hierarchical synchronization architecture preferred for large quantum processors?

options.text: It reduces the total number of cables needed
It allows different timing requirements for intra-module vs inter-module operations
It eliminates the need for calibration
It increases the master clock frequency

questions.explanation: Hierarchical architecture allows tighter synchronization within modules (where qubits frequently interact) while accepting looser synchronization between modules. Intra-module might require <1 ns timing, while inter-module might tolerate <10 ns. This makes scaling more practical.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid sources of timing jitter in a quantum control system? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Phase noise from the master oscillator
Power supply voltage ripple
Temperature-induced cable length changes
Qubit T1 relaxation time

questions.explanation: Options A, B, and C are all valid jitter sources. Phase noise from oscillators directly contributes timing uncertainty. Power supply noise modulates internal delays in electronics. Temperature changes cable propagation velocity. Option D (T1 relaxation) affects qubit coherence but does not cause control system timing jitter.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach when timing measurements show one channel consistently arriving 15 ps earlier than others?

options.text: Ignore it since 15 ps is too small to matter
Replace the master clock
Add a calibrated delay to that channel to align it with others
Shorten the cables on all other channels

questions.explanation: Adding a calibrated delay to the early channel is the correct approach. This is typically done with a programmable digital delay in the FPGA or a fine analog delay line. Shortening other cables would be impractical and expensive. 15 ps can matter for high-fidelity operations (it's ~27 degrees at 5 GHz).
